name: Czechia
tagline: From Great Moravia and the crown of Saint Wenceslas to the heart of Europe, by way of Hus, White Mountain and two peaceful revolutions.
summary: The history of the Czech lands runs from the Slavic state of Great Moravia, where Saints Cyril and Methodius first brought Christianity and a written Slavic liturgy in the ninth century, to the modern republic at the centre of the European Union. The Premyslid dukes and their patron Saint Wenceslas forged a duchy that became the Kingdom of Bohemia, which reached its zenith under Charles IV, who made Prague an imperial capital and founded central Europe's first university in 1348. Bohemia produced one of Europe's earliest religious reformations under Jan Hus, whose burning in 1415 set off the Hussite Wars; two centuries later the Defenestration of Prague in 1618 helped ignite the Thirty Years' War, and defeat at the Battle of White Mountain in 1620 brought three centuries of Habsburg and Catholic domination. A nineteenth-century National Revival rebuilt the Czech language and nation, and in 1918 the multi-ethnic state of Czechoslovakia was founded under Tomas Garrigue Masaryk. That state was dismembered by the Munich Agreement of 1938 and occupied by Nazi Germany; after the war some three million Sudeten Germans were expelled, a communist coup followed in 1948, the Prague Spring of 1968 was crushed by Warsaw Pact tanks, and the Velvet Revolution of 1989 restored democracy. On 1 January 1993 Czechoslovakia divided peacefully into the Czech Republic and Slovakia, and the new state joined NATO in 1999 and the European Union in 2004.

founding.label: The peaceful birth of the modern Czech state (the Velvet Divorce)
founding.detail: The modern Czech state came into being on 1 January 1993, when Czechoslovakia dissolved peacefully into two sovereign successor states, the Czech Republic and Slovakia, in a separation so amicable that it became known as the 'Velvet Divorce'. After the Velvet Revolution of 1989 ended communist rule, growing political and economic differences between Czech and Slovak leaders led the federal legislature to vote in November 1992 to divide the country; separate constitutions were drafted and the two republics came into existence at the start of 1993 without bloodshed, in sharp contrast to the violent breakup of Yugoslavia at the same time. The Czech statehood proclaimed in 1993 was, however, the latest chapter of a far older story: a continuity of Czech political identity that reaches back through the Kingdom of Bohemia, the duchy of the Premyslids and Saint Wenceslas, to the ninth-century Slavic state of Great Moravia.

quickFacts.label: Earliest Slavic state
quickFacts.value: Great Moravia (9th century); mission of Saints Cyril and Methodius from 863

quickFacts.label: Kingdom of Bohemia
quickFacts.value: Royal title made hereditary by the Golden Bull of Sicily, 1212; golden age under Charles IV

quickFacts.label: Charles University
quickFacts.value: Founded in Prague by Charles IV in 1348, the first university in central Europe

quickFacts.label: Czechoslovakia founded
quickFacts.value: Independence proclaimed in Prague on 28 October 1918 under Tomas Garrigue Masaryk

quickFacts.label: Modern republic
quickFacts.value: Czech Republic created by the peaceful 'Velvet Divorce' on 1 January 1993

quickFacts.label: NATO and the EU
quickFacts.value: Joined NATO on 12 March 1999 and the European Union on 1 May 2004

eras.title: Great Moravia, Saint Wenceslas and the Premyslid Dukes
eras.standfirst: From the first Slavic state of Great Moravia and the mission of Cyril and Methodius rose the Premyslid dukes of Bohemia and their martyred patron, Saint Wenceslas.
body: The earliest substantial state in the Czech lands was Great Moravia, a Slavic realm that emerged in the ninth century in the valleys of the Morava and Danube. Its decisive moment came in 862, when Prince Rostislav asked Constantinople for missionaries who could preach in a tongue his people understood and counter Frankish ecclesiastical influence. About 863 the Byzantine emperor sent the brothers Cyril and Methodius, natives of Thessalonica fluent in a Slavic dialect, who translated the Christian liturgy into what became Old Church Slavonic and devised the Glagolitic alphabet, the first script created specifically for a Slavic language. The brothers are venerated as the 'Apostles of the Slavs', and their mission anchored the Czech lands to Christendom even though Great Moravia itself collapsed under Magyar pressure in the early tenth century.

As Great Moravia fell, leadership of the western Slavs passed to the dukes of Bohemia of the Premyslid line, centred on Prague. The most celebrated of these early rulers was Wenceslas (Vaclav), duke of Bohemia, a pious Christian prince who encouraged the work of missionary priests. Murdered by his brother Boleslav about 929 (some accounts place the killing in 935), Wenceslas was almost immediately regarded as a martyr and became the patron saint of the Czech nation; the crown of Bohemia would later be known as the Crown of Saint Wenceslas, the symbol of Czech statehood.

Over the following centuries the Premyslids consolidated their duchy, balanced between independence and the orbit of the Holy Roman Empire. They extended Christianity, founded bishoprics and built Prague into a centre of trade and power. By the late twelfth and early thirteenth centuries the dukes had won recognition as kings, and in 1212 the Golden Bull of Sicily, issued by Emperor Frederick II, confirmed the royal title as hereditary, raising Bohemia from a duchy to a kingdom within the Empire.

events.title: Mission of Cyril and Methodius to Great Moravia
events.summary: At the request of Prince Rostislav, the Byzantine brothers Cyril and Methodius came to Great Moravia, translated the liturgy into Old Church Slavonic and devised the Glagolitic alphabet, bringing Christianity to the Slavs.

events.title: Fall of Great Moravia
events.summary: Weakened by internal division and overwhelmed by the Magyars, Great Moravia disintegrated in the early tenth century, and leadership of the western Slavs shifted to the Premyslid dukes of Bohemia.

events.title: Martyrdom of Saint Wenceslas
events.summary: Wenceslas (Vaclav), Premyslid duke of Bohemia and promoter of Christianity, was murdered by his brother and was at once venerated as a martyr, becoming the patron saint of the Czech nation.

events.title: Golden Bull of Sicily and the Kingdom of Bohemia
events.summary: The Golden Bull of Sicily, issued by Emperor Frederick II, confirmed the royal title of the Premyslid rulers as hereditary, formally raising Bohemia from a duchy to a kingdom within the Holy Roman Empire.

figures.name: Saints Cyril and Methodius
figures.blurb: Brothers from Thessalonica sent to Great Moravia about 863, they translated Christian texts into Old Church Slavonic and created the Glagolitic alphabet, the first script for a Slavic language, shaping the religious and cultural life of the Slavs.

figures.name: Saint Wenceslas (Vaclav)
figures.role: Duke of Bohemia and patron saint
figures.blurb: A devout Premyslid duke who fostered Christianity in Bohemia, Wenceslas was murdered by his brother and venerated as a martyr almost at once. He became the patron saint of the Czech nation, and the royal crown of Bohemia bears his name.

pullquote.text: Cyril and Methodius are venerated as the Apostles of the Slavs, having given the Slavic peoples both Christianity and their first written language.
pullquote.attribution: On the mission to Great Moravia

eras.title: The Golden Age of Bohemia under Charles IV
eras.standfirst: Under the Luxembourg king and emperor Charles IV, Prague became an imperial capital, a Gothic city of cathedral, bridge and university at the very heart of Europe.
body: After the extinction of the Premyslid male line in 1306, the Bohemian crown passed to the House of Luxembourg. From this line came the realm's greatest sovereign, Charles IV, king of Bohemia from 1346 and Holy Roman Emperor from 1355. Raised at the French court and deeply attached to his Czech kingdom, Charles made Prague not merely a royal seat but the de facto capital of the Holy Roman Empire, ushering in what is remembered as the golden age of Bohemia.

Charles transformed Prague into one of the great cities of Christendom. In 1348 he founded the university that bears his name, Charles University, the first university in central Europe, granted the same rights and liberties as Paris and Bologna. He laid out the New Town of Prague, began the Gothic St Vitus Cathedral within Prague Castle, and ordered the building of the stone bridge across the Vltava, the Charles Bridge, that still carries his name. These works form the core of the Historic Centre of Prague, inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage site in 1992 for the architectural influence the city radiated across Europe from the Middle Ages.

Charles also gave the Empire its enduring constitution, the Golden Bull of 1356, which fixed the procedure for electing the emperor and confirmed the king of Bohemia among the seven prince-electors. The wealth that financed this flowering came in part from the silver mines of Kutna Hora, a royal mining town whose prosperity in the fourteenth century endowed it with monuments such as the great Church of St Barbara; Kutna Hora was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1995. By Charles's death in 1378 the Kingdom of Bohemia stood at the summit of its medieval power and prestige.

events.title: End of the Premyslid dynasty
events.summary: The murder of Wenceslas III extinguished the male line of the Premyslids; after a brief contest the Bohemian crown passed to the House of Luxembourg, the dynasty of Charles IV.

events.title: Foundation of Charles University
events.summary: Charles IV founded the university in Prague that bears his name, the first university in central Europe, endowed with the same rights and liberties as the universities of Paris and Bologna.

events.title: Charles IV crowned Holy Roman Emperor
events.summary: Already king of Bohemia, Charles was crowned Holy Roman Emperor in Rome in 1355, making Prague the principal residence of the Empire and the centre of European politics and culture.

events.title: Building of Charles Bridge and Gothic Prague
events.summary: Charles IV laid the foundation of the stone bridge over the Vltava and advanced St Vitus Cathedral and the New Town, creating the Gothic core of Prague later recognised as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

figures.name: Charles IV
figures.role: King of Bohemia and Holy Roman Emperor
figures.blurb: The greatest of Bohemia's medieval rulers, Charles IV made Prague an imperial capital, founded Charles University in 1348, built the cathedral and bridge that define the city, and issued the Golden Bull of 1356 governing imperial elections.

figures.name: Arnost of Pardubice
figures.blurb: Confidant and adviser of Charles IV, Arnost became the first archbishop of Prague when the see was elevated in 1344 and served as the founding chancellor of Charles University in 1348, helping to make Prague an ecclesiastical and intellectual centre.

pullquote.text: The magnificent monuments of Prague, including St Vitus Cathedral and Charles Bridge, were built mostly in the 14th century under the Holy Roman Emperor Charles IV.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO World Heritage Centre, Historic Centre of Prague

eras.title: Jan Hus, the Hussite Wars and the Bohemian Reformation
eras.standfirst: A century before Luther, the Prague preacher Jan Hus challenged the Church; his burning in 1415 unleashed the Hussite Wars, in which Bohemia defied crusade after crusade.
body: Out of Charles IV's university came the most influential figure of the Czech Middle Ages, the reformer Jan Hus. A preacher at the Bethlehem Chapel in Prague and rector of the university, Hus drew on the ideas of the English theologian John Wycliffe to attack clerical corruption, the sale of indulgences and the wealth of the Church, and insisted on the authority of Scripture and preaching in the vernacular. His work stands between the medieval and the Reformation eras and made him a national as well as a religious figure.

Summoned to the Council of Constance under a promise of safe conduct, Hus was condemned as a heretic and burned at the stake on 6 July 1415. Far from silencing the movement, his death gave the Czech reformers a martyr. Their cause, henceforth called Hussite rather than Wycliffite, spread rapidly and demanded, among other things, communion in both kinds, bread and wine, for the laity, the symbol that gave the Utraquists their name.

Between 1419 and the 1430s the Hussites fought a series of wars against the Catholic king and a succession of papal crusades. Under the brilliant blind commander Jan Zizka and his successors, the Hussite armies, with their war-wagon tactics and fervour, repeatedly destroyed the forces sent against them. The conflict ended in compromise: in 1436 the moderate Utraquist Hussites secured the Compact of Iglau (Jihlava), which conceded communion in both kinds and an independent Bohemian church. Bohemia remained largely under Hussite influence until the crown passed to the Catholic Habsburgs in 1526, an event that set the stage for the religious confrontation of the next century.

events.title: Jan Hus preaches at the Bethlehem Chapel
events.summary: Appointed preacher at the Bethlehem Chapel in Prague, Hus began to denounce church corruption and indulgences and to champion reform and vernacular preaching, drawing on the ideas of John Wycliffe.

events.title: Jan Hus burned at the Council of Constance
events.summary: Condemned as a heretic despite a promise of safe conduct, Hus was burned at the stake at Constance on 6 July 1415, becoming a martyr whose death set off the Hussite movement.

events.title: The Hussite Wars
events.summary: Followers of Hus fought a series of wars against the Catholic crown and papal crusades; under Jan Zizka the Hussite armies proved nearly invincible before the conflict ended in compromise.

events.title: Compact of Iglau
events.summary: The moderate Utraquist Hussites secured a peace settlement, the Compact of Iglau, that granted communion in both kinds and an independent Bohemian Catholic church, ending the main phase of the wars.

figures.name: Jan Hus
figures.blurb: The foremost Czech reformer of the fifteenth century, Hus preached against church corruption and for vernacular Scripture a century before Luther. Burned at the Council of Constance in 1415, he became a national martyr and gave his name to the Hussite movement.

figures.name: Jan Zizka
figures.blurb: The general of the radical Hussites, Zizka pioneered the use of fortified war-wagons and fielded a disciplined peasant army that defeated crusade after crusade, fighting on even after he was completely blind, and was never beaten in the field.

pullquote.text: By killing Hus, the church authorities provided the Czech reformers with a martyr; from then on the movement took the name Hussite and grew rapidly.

eras.title: Habsburg Bohemia: White Mountain, Counter-Reformation and National Revival
eras.standfirst: The Defenestration of Prague helped ignite the Thirty Years' War; defeat at White Mountain brought three centuries of Habsburg rule, against which a nineteenth-century revival rebuilt the Czech nation.
body: When the Bohemian estates elected the Habsburg Ferdinand I king in 1526, Bohemia entered a personal union with Austria and Hungary that would bind it to the Habsburg monarchy for almost four centuries. Tension between the largely Protestant Czech nobility and their Catholic Habsburg rulers grew acute under Emperor Ferdinand II. On 23 May 1618 Protestant nobles, angered by the closing of Protestant chapels in breach of the Letter of Majesty of 1609, threw two imperial regents and their secretary from a window of Prague Castle, the Defenestration of Prague. The men survived, but the act launched a Bohemian revolt that became one of the opening acts of the Thirty Years' War.

The revolt was crushed at the Battle of White Mountain near Prague on 8 November 1620, where the Catholic and imperial armies destroyed the rebel forces in barely an hour. Defeat was catastrophic for the Czech estates: rebel leaders were executed in Prague's Old Town Square in 1621, Protestant nobles were dispossessed or exiled, and the kingdom was subjected to forced re-Catholicisation and the consolidation of Habsburg absolutism. The period that followed, in which German became dominant in public life and the Czech-speaking elite shrank, is remembered in Czech tradition as the 'Time of Darkness'.

Yet the Czech nation was reborn through culture rather than arms. From the late eighteenth century the Czech National Revival set out to rescue and codify the Czech language and to reconstruct Czech history and identity. The historian Frantisek Palacky, sometimes called the 'Father of the Nation', wrote a monumental history of the Czech people and made the Hussite age the centre of national memory; writers, scholars and musicians rebuilt a modern Czech literature and public life. By the later nineteenth century this cultural revival had become a political national movement within the Austro-Hungarian Empire, demanding recognition and autonomy for the Czechs.

events.title: Bohemia under the Habsburgs
events.summary: The Bohemian estates elected Ferdinand I of Habsburg as king, binding the kingdom to the Habsburg monarchy and to Austria and Hungary for nearly four centuries.

events.title: Defenestration of Prague
events.summary: Protestant nobles threw two Catholic imperial regents and their secretary from a window of Prague Castle, sparking the Bohemian revolt against Ferdinand II and helping to launch the Thirty Years' War.

events.title: Battle of White Mountain
events.summary: Imperial and Catholic League armies crushed the Bohemian rebels at White Mountain near Prague, ending Czech estates' power and ushering in Habsburg absolutism and forced Counter-Reformation.

events.title: The Czech National Revival
events.summary: From the late 18th century, scholars led by the historian Frantisek Palacky rebuilt and codified the Czech language and national history, transforming a cultural awakening into a political movement within Austria-Hungary.

figures.name: Frantisek Palacky
figures.blurb: The founder of modern Czech historiography, Palacky wrote a vast history of the Czech people, placed the Hussite era at the heart of national memory, and became the leading political voice of the nineteenth-century National Revival.

figures.name: Ferdinand II
figures.role: Habsburg Holy Roman Emperor and king of Bohemia
figures.blurb: A zealous Catholic, Ferdinand II provoked the Bohemian revolt of 1618 and, after the victory at White Mountain in 1620, imposed Counter-Reformation and absolutist rule on the Czech lands.

pullquote.text: The Defenestration of Prague of 1618 was the signal for the beginning of a Bohemian revolt against the Habsburgs, marking one of the opening phases of the Thirty Years' War.

eras.title: Czechoslovakia, the Munich Betrayal and Nazi Occupation
eras.standfirst: Masaryk's multi-ethnic republic was Europe's surviving interwar democracy until the Munich Agreement of 1938 ceded the Sudetenland to Hitler and Nazi Germany occupied the Czech lands.
body: As Austria-Hungary collapsed at the end of the First World War, an independent Czechoslovakia was proclaimed in Prague on 28 October 1918. Its chief architect was Tomas Garrigue Masaryk, who from exile had organised the Czechoslovak National Council in Paris and won Allied recognition for the cause; he became the new republic's first president. The state united the historic lands of Bohemia, Moravia and Silesia with Slovakia and Subcarpathian Ruthenia, and was a genuine multi-ethnic democracy. But it also contained some three million ethnic Germans, concentrated in the border region known as the Sudetenland, a fact Adolf Hitler would exploit.

In 1938 Hitler demanded the cession of the Sudetenland, threatening war. At Munich on 29-30 September 1938, the leaders of Germany, Italy, Britain and France agreed that Czechoslovakia must surrender its German-majority borderlands and defences to Germany. Czechoslovakia, the country whose fate was being decided, was not even a party to the negotiations and gave way only under intense pressure from Britain and France, an abandonment long remembered in Czech history as the 'Munich betrayal'. German troops occupied the Sudetenland in the first days of October 1938.

Munich was the prelude to destruction. On 15 March 1939 German forces occupied the Czech lands in violation of the agreement, and Hitler proclaimed the Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia, while a separate clerical-fascist Slovak state was set up under German tutelage. Within little more than two decades of its founding, Czechoslovakia had vanished from the map. The occupation brought repression, forced labour and, for the country's Jews, deportation and murder: some 263,000 Jews from the prewar Czechoslovak Republic were killed in the Holocaust.

events.title: Foundation of Czechoslovakia
events.summary: With Austria-Hungary collapsing, independence was proclaimed in Prague on 28 October 1918; Tomas Garrigue Masaryk became the first president of the new multi-ethnic democratic republic.

events.title: The Munich Agreement
events.summary: Germany, Italy, Britain and France agreed at Munich that Czechoslovakia must cede the German-majority Sudetenland to Nazi Germany; Czechoslovakia was not a party and yielded under Anglo-French pressure.

events.title: German annexation of the Sudetenland
events.summary: Following the Munich Agreement, German forces occupied the Sudetenland, stripping Czechoslovakia of its border defences and around three million inhabitants in its German-speaking borderlands.

events.title: Occupation and the Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia
events.summary: In breach of Munich, Germany occupied the Czech lands on 15 March 1939 and proclaimed the Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia, while a separate Slovak state was set up under German control.

figures.name: Tomas Garrigue Masaryk
figures.role: Founder and first President of Czechoslovakia
figures.blurb: Philosopher and statesman, Masaryk organised the campaign for Czechoslovak independence from exile during the First World War and won Allied recognition. Proclaimed president in 1918, he became the moral founder of the interwar republic.

figures.name: Edvard Benes
figures.blurb: Masaryk's close collaborator and foreign minister, Benes succeeded him as president in 1935. He resigned after Munich, led the government-in-exile during the war and returned to office in 1945, issuing the decrees that expelled the Sudeten Germans.

pullquote.text: The agreement required Czechoslovakia to surrender its border regions and defences to Nazi Germany; the country was excluded from the negotiations and agreed only under significant pressure from Britain and France.
pullquote.attribution: United States Holocaust Memorial Museum, on the Munich Agreement

eras.title: Expulsion, Communism, the Velvet Revolution and the Modern Republic
eras.standfirst: After the expulsion of the Sudeten Germans and a communist coup, the Prague Spring was crushed by tanks; the Velvet Revolution then restored democracy and the Velvet Divorce created the Czech Republic.
body: Liberation in 1945 was followed by upheaval. Under presidential decrees issued by Edvard Benes and sanctioned by the Allies at Potsdam, the citizenship and property of the country's Germans were stripped, and around three million Sudeten Germans were forcibly expelled, the largest population movement in the history of the Czech lands. Reckoning with the wartime occupation, the expulsion permanently reshaped the borderlands and remains a sensitive subject in Czech-German relations. The fragile postwar democracy did not last: in February 1948 the Communist Party seized full power in a coup, bringing Czechoslovakia firmly into the Soviet bloc.

Two decades of Stalinist rule gave way in 1968 to an attempt at reform. Under Alexander Dubcek the Communist leadership pursued 'socialism with a human face', relaxing censorship and opening political debate in the movement known as the Prague Spring. Moscow would not tolerate it: on the night of 20-21 August 1968 some half a million Warsaw Pact troops, led by the Soviet Union, invaded Czechoslovakia, crushing the reforms and imposing the harsh 'normalisation' that followed. Resistance never wholly died, sustained by dissidents such as the playwright Vaclav Havel and the Charter 77 movement.

The end came swiftly in 1989. A student demonstration in Prague on 17 November was beaten by police, igniting mass protests; within weeks the communist regime collapsed in the peaceful Velvet Revolution, and on 29 December 1989 Havel was elected president. Czech and Slovak leaders could not agree on the shape of their shared state, and on 1 January 1993 Czechoslovakia divided peacefully into the Czech Republic and Slovakia, the 'Velvet Divorce'. The new republic completed its return to the West by joining NATO on 12 March 1999 and the European Union on 1 May 2004.

events.title: Expulsion of the Sudeten Germans
events.summary: Under the Benes decrees and Allied agreement at Potsdam, Czechoslovakia stripped the citizenship and property of its Germans and expelled some three million Sudeten Germans, the largest forced migration in the history of the Czech lands.

events.title: Communist coup
events.summary: In February 1948 the Communist Party seized full power in Czechoslovakia, ending postwar democracy and bringing the country firmly into the Soviet bloc for the next four decades.

events.title: Prague Spring crushed by Warsaw Pact
events.summary: Alexander Dubcek's reforms of 'socialism with a human face' were ended when roughly half a million Warsaw Pact troops, led by the Soviet Union, invaded Czechoslovakia in August 1968.

events.title: The Velvet Revolution
events.summary: A suppressed student march in Prague on 17 November 1989 set off mass protests that toppled the communist regime within weeks; Vaclav Havel was elected president on 29 December.

events.title: The Velvet Divorce and birth of the Czech Republic
events.summary: After a 1992 vote to divide, Czechoslovakia split peacefully on 1 January 1993 into the Czech Republic and Slovakia; the Czech Republic later joined NATO in 1999 and the EU in 2004.

figures.name: Vaclav Havel
figures.blurb: A playwright imprisoned for his dissent and a founder of Charter 77, Havel became the moral leader of the Velvet Revolution. Elected president of Czechoslovakia in 1989, he served as the first president of the Czech Republic from 1993 to 2003.

figures.name: Alexander Dubcek
figures.role: Reform Communist leader of the Prague Spring
figures.blurb: As first secretary of the Communist Party in 1968, Dubcek led the Prague Spring reforms aimed at 'socialism with a human face'. The Warsaw Pact invasion ended the experiment, but he re-emerged in 1989 as a symbol of the Velvet Revolution.

pullquote.text: Like the Velvet Revolution of 1989, the partition was entirely without bloodshed and so was dubbed the Velvet Divorce.
pullquote.attribution: On the peaceful dissolution of Czechoslovakia in 1993

figures.name: Charles IV
figures.role: King of Bohemia and Holy Roman Emperor
figures.blurb: Bohemia's greatest medieval ruler, who made Prague an imperial capital, founded Charles University in 1348 and built the Gothic city of cathedral and bridge that survives as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

figures.name: Jan Hus
figures.blurb: Czech reformer burned at the Council of Constance in 1415, whose challenge to the Church a century before Luther triggered the Hussite Wars and made him an enduring national martyr.

figures.name: Tomas Garrigue Masaryk
figures.role: Founder and first President of Czechoslovakia
figures.blurb: Philosopher-statesman who led the wartime campaign for independence and became the founding president of the Czechoslovak republic proclaimed in 1918.

figures.name: Vaclav Havel
figures.blurb: Leader of the Velvet Revolution, last president of Czechoslovakia and first president of the Czech Republic, the moral face of the country's peaceful return to democracy.

sources.label: Wenceslas I, prince of Bohemia (Saint Wenceslas)
Kutna Hora: Historical Town Centre with the Church of St Barbara and the Cathedral of Our Lady at Sedlec
Czechoslovak history: National awakening and the rise of constitutionalism
